Rosyth School

Primary 6 Science — Weighted Assessment 1 (Term Assessment 2024, Term 1)

Booklet A: 28 multiple-choice questions (56 marks)  •  Booklet B: 12 open-ended questions (44 marks)  •  Total: 100 marks  •  Duration: 1 h 45 min

Booklet A  ·  Multiple Choice (Questions 1–28)

For each question, four options are given. One of them is the correct answer.

Question 1

2 marks

Which one of the following characteristics can be used to differentiate between a reptile and a bird?

  1. The way they breathe
  2. The way they reproduce
  3. The number of body parts
  4. The type of outer covering

Answer

Option 4. Both reptiles and birds breathe with lungs, reproduce by laying eggs and have the same number of body parts, so these cannot tell them apart. The key difference is the outer covering: reptiles have scales while birds have feathers.

Question 2

2 marks

All plants ____________.

  1. reproduce by seeds
  2. make their own food
  3. bear flowers and fruits
  4. need oxygen at night only

Answer

Option 2. All plants contain chlorophyll and make their own food by photosynthesis. Not all plants reproduce by seeds (ferns and mosses use spores), not all bear flowers and fruits (non-flowering plants), and plants need oxygen for respiration at all times, not only at night.

Question 3

2 marks

Which characteristic(s) can be used to classify a tomato plant, a bird nest fern and a mushroom into two groups?

A: Ways of reproduction
B: Ways of obtaining food
C: Flowering or non-flowering plants

  1. A only
  2. A and B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 2. By reproduction (A): the tomato plant uses seeds while the fern and mushroom use spores. By way of obtaining food (B): the tomato and fern make their own food while the mushroom cannot. Both A and B split them into two groups. C fails because a mushroom is a fungus, not a non-flowering plant, so the flowering/non-flowering grouping does not apply to all three.

Question 4

2 marks

A group of living things was observed under a microscope over three days.

Which characteristic of living things was shown over the three days?

  1. Living things can die.
  2. Living things can grow.
  3. Living things can respond.
  4. Living things can reproduce.

Answer

Option 3. The same organisms move within the dish over the three days, shifting their position towards the sunlight coming from the top. Moving in response to a stimulus (light) shows that living things can respond. Their number does not increase, so it is not reproduction.

Question 5

2 marks

Many years ago, a scientist discovered an animal P and studied its characteristics. Some of the characteristics of animal P are as follows:

A: lays eggs
B: has four legs
C: can swim in water
D: can produce milk to feed the young

Which of the above characteristics made it difficult to classify animal P as a mammal?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 1. A defining feature of mammals is that they give birth to live young, not lay eggs. Animal P lays eggs (characteristic A), which is unusual for a mammal, making it hard to classify (like the platypus). Having four legs, swimming and producing milk are all consistent with being a mammal.

Question 6

2 marks

Observe the flow chart below.

Which of the organisms above is likely a mushroom?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 4. Following the chart for a mushroom: it cannot make its own food (No), so it goes to the next question; it can break down dead matter into simpler substances (Yes — a fungus is a decomposer); and it can be seen with the naked eye (Yes). This path leads to box D.

Question 7

2 marks

Study the life cycle of an animal.

Which of the following correctly shows the animal and stage Q?

AnimalQ
(1)butterflyegg
(2)mosquitoadult
(3)cockroachadult
(4)frogegg

Answer

Option 1. Fertilisation occurs at P, and the next stage Q (just after the egg is fertilised) is the egg stage. A four-stage cycle (egg → larva → pupa → adult) matches a butterfly, with Q being the egg. The frog has no pupa stage, and for mosquito/cockroach Q would not be the adult.

Question 8

2 marks

Study the classification chart below.

The diagram below shows the young and adult of insect A.

Which group, P, Q, R or S, does insect A belong to?

  1. P
  2. Q
  3. R
  4. S

Answer

Option 3. The insect shown is a fly, which has a four-stage life cycle (egg → larva → pupa → adult) and the adult has wings. Following the chart: four stages → adult with wings → group R.

Question 9

2 marks

Han Wei carried out an experiment to find out if the temperature of the surroundings affects the life cycle of an organism. He recorded his observation in the table below.

Temperature of the surrounding (°C)Number of days for one complete cycle
1545
2032
2525
3018

Which of the following conclusion is correct for Han Wei's experiment?

  1. The organism does not survive at 30°C.
  2. The organism has four stages in its life cycle at 15°C.
  3. The organism takes a shorter time to complete one life cycle when the temperature of the surroundings is lower.
  4. The organism takes a longer time to complete one life cycle when the temperature of the surroundings is lower.

Answer

Option 4. As the temperature falls from 30°C to 15°C, the number of days for one complete cycle rises from 18 to 45. So a lower temperature means a longer time to complete the life cycle. The table gives no information about survival or number of stages.

Question 10

2 marks

Which of the following statements about matter is/are true?

A: Matter has mass.
B: Matter occupies space.
C: Matter has a definite volume.

  1. A only
  2. B and C only
  3. A and B only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3. All matter has mass and occupies space (A and B are always true). However, matter does not always have a definite volume — gases take the volume of their container, so C is not true for all matter.

Question 11

2 marks

Air is pumped into the balloon as shown below using an air pump. The left picture shows the balloon after 3 pumps of air; the right picture shows it after 2 more pumps of air.

What happens to the total volume and the mass of air in the balloon after 2 more pumps of air is given?

Total volume of air in the balloonTotal mass of air in the balloon
(1)remains the sameincreases
(2)increasesincreases
(3)remains the sameremains the same
(4)increasesremains the same

Answer

Option 2. Pumping in more air adds more air particles, so the balloon gets bigger (volume increases) and there is more air inside (mass increases). Both quantities increase.

Question 12

2 marks

Ahmad was feeling cold as he was walking in a cool windy place. Then he sat down resting his bottom on a rock. Immediately, his bottom felt colder.

His bottom felt colder because it

  1. lost heat to the rock
  2. did not gain heat from the rock
  3. lost more heat to the surrounding air
  4. is not exposed to the surrounding air

Answer

Option 1. The rock is colder than his body, so heat flows from his warmer body to the cooler rock. Losing heat to the rock is what makes his bottom feel colder.

Question 13

2 marks

Two liquids, A and B, of the same amount, are first heated and left to cool in a classroom. The graph below shows the temperature of two liquids, A and B, during the cooling process.

Which one of the following statements is true?

  1. Liquid A boils at 40°C.
  2. Both liquids melt at 10°C.
  3. Liquid A has a higher boiling point than Liquid B.
  4. The temperature of the classroom is below 20°C.

Answer

Option 4. Both liquids cool and level off (reach room temperature) at about 13–16°C, which is below 20°C, so the classroom temperature is below 20°C. The graph shows cooling, not boiling or melting, so options 1–3 cannot be concluded.

Question 14

2 marks

There is a lighted candle and a toy behind a screen.

Which of the following explains why Nadiah cannot see the toy?

  1. No light is entering Nadiah's eyes.
  2. The toy is not reflecting any light.
  3. Only some light can pass through the screen.
  4. No light is reflected from the toy into Nadiah's eyes.

Answer

Option 4. We see an object when light reflected from it enters our eyes. The screen blocks the toy, so no light reflected from the toy reaches Nadiah's eyes and she cannot see it. Light still enters her eyes from elsewhere (so option 1 is wrong), and the toy does reflect light (option 2 is wrong).

Question 15

2 marks

Johnson listed some sources of energy and their forms of energy. Which one of the following is not correct?

Source of energyForm of energy
(1)BatteryElectrical
(2)SunHeat and Light
(3)FoodPotential
(4)WindKinetic

Answer

Option 1. A battery is a store of chemical (potential) energy, not electrical energy — it converts chemical energy into electrical energy when used. The other three pairings are correct: the Sun gives heat and light, food stores chemical potential energy, and wind is moving air (kinetic energy).

Question 16

2 marks

Ravinder had two identical solid cylinders. He drilled a hole through one of the cylinders and made it hollow in the middle. Then he released both cylinders, hollow and solid, from the top of the ramp as shown below.

Which one of the following is the same for both the cylinders in the experiment?

  1. Gravitational force acting on both cylinders.
  2. Potential energy possessed by the cylinders at the top of the ramp.
  3. Friction between the surface of the cylinders and the surface of the ramp.
  4. Kinetic energy possessed by the cylinders when the cylinders are resting on the ground.

Answer

Option 4. When the cylinders are at rest on the ground they are not moving, so each has zero kinetic energy — the same for both. Drilling a hole removes material, so the hollow cylinder has less mass; this gives it a smaller gravitational force, less potential energy and different friction. Only the kinetic energy at rest (zero) is the same.

Question 17

2 marks

Study the energy conversion below.

Kinetic energy → Electrical energy

Which one of the following can represent the energy conversion correctly?

  1. Using a handphone
  2. Switching on a television
  3. Spinning a bicycle dynamo
  4. Typing on a computer keyboard

Answer

Option 3. A bicycle dynamo is turned by the moving wheel (kinetic energy) and generates electricity (electrical energy) to power a lamp. This matches kinetic → electrical. The other options mainly convert electrical energy into other forms.

Question 18

2 marks

The table shows the percentage of useful energy released from three types of batteries, R, S and T. Each battery is charged to 100 units.

BatteryPercentage of useful energy (%)
R70
S85
T90

The batteries are used in similar electric cars to move them. Using the information above, which statement is correct?

  1. Battery T generates the most amount of heat energy.
  2. Battery T will take the longest time to charge to 100 units.
  3. Battery R converts the potential energy to least electrical energy.
  4. Battery S will allow the electric car to travel more distance than Battery T.

Answer

Option 3. Battery R releases the lowest percentage of useful energy (70%), so it converts the least of its stored energy into useful (electrical) energy, with the rest wasted as heat. Battery T is the most efficient (90% useful, least heat wasted) and would let the car travel furthest, so options 1 and 4 are wrong.

Question 19

2 marks

A ball is thrown as shown below (points K, L and M along its path).

Which graph shows the kinetic energy of the ball at points K, L and M?

Answer

Option 1. Kinetic energy depends on how fast the ball is moving, which depends on its height: the higher the point, the slower the ball and the less kinetic energy it has. Along the path the ball is highest at K and gets progressively lower through L to M, so it speeds up and its kinetic energy increases from K to L to M. Graph (1) is the only one showing kinetic energy rising K < L < M.

Question 20

2 marks

Study the diagram below. Energy conversion takes place as the cyclist moves from A to C. He stops at B for a minute before coming down.

Which one of the following shows the main form(s) of energy possessed by the cyclist as he moved from A to C respectively?

ABC
(1)Kinetic + PotentialPotential onlyKinetic only
(2)Kinetic + PotentialPotential onlyKinetic + Potential
(3)Kinetic onlyPotential onlyKinetic + Potential
(4)Kinetic + HeatPotential + KineticKinetic + Heat

Answer

Option 2. At A the cyclist is moving up the slope, so he has both kinetic and (rising) potential energy. At B he has stopped at the top, so only potential energy. At C he is part-way down and still above the ground while moving, so he has both kinetic and potential energy.

Question 21

2 marks

Keri was carrying a stack of books and a bouncy ball. As she was walking, the bouncy ball fell to the ground. When it hits the ground at B, the ball changes its shape and then it bounces to C.

What is the energy conversion when the ball hits the ground at B?

  1. kinetic → sound + heat
  2. gravitational potential → kinetic + sound
  3. kinetic → heat + sound + elastic potential
  4. kinetic → sound + heat + elastic potential

Answer

Option 2. As the ball drops and hits the ground at B, its stored gravitational potential energy is converted into the energy of motion (kinetic energy) together with the sound energy produced on impact, i.e. gravitational potential → kinetic + sound.

Question 22

2 marks

The diagram below shows a phone holder for a car. Material X is pasted on the phone cover. The phone with the cover is then safely secured on the phone holder in the car.

Which of the following statement is definitely correct?

  1. Material X must be a magnet.
  2. Material X cannot be a magnet.
  3. Material X is made of a magnetic material.
  4. Material X is made of non-magnetic material.

Answer

Option 3. The holder contains a magnet that attracts material X to hold the phone. For X to be attracted to a magnet, it must be made of a magnetic material (such as iron or steel). X itself need not be a magnet — a magnetic (but non-magnet) material would also be attracted — so only “made of a magnetic material” is definitely correct.

Question 23

2 marks

Zaki carried out an experiment with three different bars, X, Y and Z. The ends of the bars are labelled as shown.

The table below shows how the bars interact when different ends of the bars are brought close to each other.

End of barEnd of barResult
A BC DRepel
A BD CAttract
A BE FAttract
A BF EAttract

Based on the results given above, which of the following is definitely true?

  1. Bar Z is made of steel.
  2. Only bar Y is a magnet.
  3. Bar X and bar Y are magnets.
  4. Bar X and bar Z are not magnets.

Answer

Option 3. Bar X (A–B) repels Bar Y in one orientation (A–B with C–D) and attracts it when Y is flipped (A–B with D–C). Repulsion can only happen between two magnets, so both X and Y are magnets. Bar Z is only ever attracted to X (both orientations), which means Z is a magnetic material but not necessarily a magnet, so we cannot say it is steel or rule it out as a magnet.

Question 24

2 marks

Stacie throws a ball downwards to hit on the net. Then the ball bounces up after it has hit the net.

What force causes the ball to bounce up after it has hit the net?

  1. pulling force from the ball
  2. pulling force from the net
  3. pushing force from the ball
  4. pushing force from the net

Answer

Option 4. The stretched net springs back and pushes the ball upward. This pushing force from the net is what makes the ball bounce up.

Question 25

2 marks

Which of the following is not an effect of force?

  1. Change in state
  2. Change in shape
  3. Change in speed
  4. Change in direction

Answer

Option 1. A force can change an object's shape, speed or direction. A change in state (e.g. solid to liquid) is caused by gaining or losing heat, not by a force, so it is not an effect of force.

Question 26

2 marks

A toy car is stationary on a slope as shown below.

Which of the following is correct about the force acting on the toy car when it is stationary on the slope? ____________ is acting on the toy car.

  1. No force
  2. Only frictional force
  3. Only gravitational force
  4. Frictional and gravitational force

Answer

Option 4. Gravity pulls the car down the slope, while friction acts up the slope to keep it from sliding. The car stays still because these two forces are balanced, so both frictional and gravitational forces act on it.

Question 27

2 marks

Simon wanted to move a box up a ramp. He pushed the box up the ramp but it did not move at all.

Why did the box not move up the ramp?

  1. There was no force acting on the box.
  2. A force smaller than the frictional force was applied.
  3. Frictional force was acting upwards along the ramp.
  4. Gravitational force was acting downwards along the ramp.

Answer

Option 2. The box does not move because Simon's push is not large enough to overcome the friction (and gravity) acting against it — the applied force is smaller than the resisting frictional force, so the forces are balanced and the box stays still.

Question 28

2 marks

A group of students made a structure as shown below to drop an egg from a height without breaking the egg.

The structure helps to ____________.

  1. increase air resistance
  2. decrease gravitational force
  3. increase the speed at which it hits the ground
  4. decrease the friction between the egg and the ground

Answer

Option 1. The parachute has a large surface area that increases air resistance as it falls, slowing the descent so the egg lands gently and does not break. Gravitational force is unchanged, and the structure slows (not speeds up) the fall.

Booklet B  ·  Open-ended (Questions 29–40)

Write your answers in the space provided. (44 marks)

Question 29

4 marks

The chart below shows a classification of living things.

(a) Write a suitable heading for group B. [1]
(b) Name an example for F. [1]
(c) State two ways group B depends on group A to stay alive. [2]

Answer

(a) Heading: Cannot make their own food (they feed on other living things). Group A makes its own food, so group B is the opposite — it cannot make its own food.
(b) Mould (a fungus that reproduces from spores and cannot make its own food).
(c) Group B depends on group A (the food-makers) for: (1) food — they eat group A to obtain energy; and (2) oxygen — group A releases oxygen during photosynthesis, which group B needs for respiration.

Question 30

2 marks

Neesha recorded her observations of some animals that she had found in a garden in the table below.

AnimalNumber of legsNumber of wings
P64
Q80
R22
S60
(a) Based on the information given, Neesha is very certain that P and S are insects. Do you agree? Explain why. [1]
(b) Neesha wanted to draw a checklist to differentiate living things from non-living things. She has done one characteristic (i: They respond to changes). Complete the table for (ii) with one more characteristic. [1]

Answer

(a) Yes. All insects have six legs, and both P and S have six legs, so they can be classified as insects. (The number of wings can vary, but having six legs is the key feature.)
(b) (ii) They need air, food and water to survive (any one valid characteristic of living things, e.g. they grow, they reproduce, or they need food/air/water).

Question 31

3 marks

The diagrams show the life cycles of two animals, A and B, living in a pond.

(a) State a difference between the two life cycles in terms of their stages. [1]
(b) Explain how laying many eggs helps both animals. [1]
(c) Both the young of the frog and mosquito live in water. If the pond dries up due to hot weather, explain how this would prevent the frog and mosquito from reproducing. [1]

Answer

(a) Animal A has four stages in its life cycle (egg → larva → pupa → adult) while animal B has three stages (egg → young → adult).
(b) Some eggs may be eaten by predators or fail to hatch. By laying many eggs, there is a higher chance that some will survive, hatch and grow into adults, ensuring the continuation of their kind.
(c) The young of the frog and mosquito need water to live. If the pond dries up, there is no water for the young to live in, so they would die before becoming adults and the animals cannot reproduce.

Question 32

4 marks

The diagram shows the life cycle of insect X.

(a) Explain why life cycles are important. [1]
(b) Based on the diagram above, state one difference between the young and the adult of insect X (Do not mention size, shape and colour). [1]
(c) State one similarity between the life cycle of a cockroach and that of Insect X. [1]
(d) The table shows the effect of surrounding temperature on the life cycle of insect X. State the relationship between the number of days for one complete life cycle of insect X and the surrounding temperature. [1]
Temperature (°C)Number of days for one complete life cycle
1045
2033
3028
4017

Answer

(a) Life cycles are important so that we can understand the different stages an organism goes through and they ensure the continuity of its kind (the species continues to reproduce and survive).
(b) The adult of insect X has wings while the young does not have wings.
(c) Both the cockroach and insect X have three stages in their life cycle (egg → young/nymph → adult).
(d) As the surrounding temperature increases, the number of days for one complete life cycle decreases.

Question 33

4 marks

(a) Draw and label the life cycle of a green plant in the box below. [2]

A girl grew a plant from a seed. She plotted a graph to show the changes in the mass of its seed leaf over four days as shown in the graph below. (Lines S and T are shown.)

(b) Which line, S or T, best represents the mass of the seed leaf during the experiment? Explain your answer. [1]
(c) How does the plant continue to grow well from day 4 onwards? [1]

Answer

(a) The completed life cycle of a green plant (seed → seedling/young plant → adult plant → seed):

A green plant's life cycle: a seed germinates into a seedling (young plant), which grows into an adult plant; the adult bears flowers and produces new seeds, and the cycle repeats.

(b) Line S. The seed leaf provides stored food to the growing seedling, so as the food is used up the mass of the seed leaf decreases over time — matching the falling line S.
(c) From day 4 onwards the plant has grown green leaves, so it can make its own food by photosynthesis instead of relying on the seed leaf, and continues to grow well.

Question 34

4 marks

Paul wants to test the physical property of different materials. He pours 200 ml water onto material X and measured the volume of water collected in the beaker below.

The procedure was repeated with materials Y and Z of similar size as material X. The volume of water collected was recorded.

MaterialXYZ
Volume of water collected at bottom beaker (ml)180170200
(a) What physical property of the materials was Paul testing? [1]
(b) Paul pours the water gently from beaker B to prevent water from splattering when it hits the material. What else should he do to ensure that the water hits materials X, Y and Z with the same amount of force? [1]
(c) Which material, X, Y or Z, is the most suitable to make a life jacket? Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

(a) He was testing whether the materials are waterproof / their ability to absorb water (water resistance).
(b) He should pour the water from the same height above each material every time, so the water hits X, Y and Z with the same force.
(c) Material Z. All 200 ml of water passed through and was collected, so Z did not absorb any water — it is waterproof. A waterproof material stays light and can float, keeping the wearer's head above water, making Z the most suitable for a life jacket.

Question 35

3 marks

The diagram shows a washing machine. When the door is closed and the machine is turned on, an electric motor rotates the drum for washing.

(a) State the energy conversions to wash the clothes. [2]

Study the graph below carefully.

(b) What is the relationship between the temperature of water at which the clothes are washed and the usage of energy? [1]
(c) Explain the relationship in (b) using energy conversion. [1]

Answer

(a) Electrical energy → kinetic energy + sound energy + heat energy (the motor turns the drum, producing movement, with some sound and heat as well).
(b) As the temperature of the water at which the clothes are washed increases, the usage of energy per wash cycle increases.
(c) To wash at a higher water temperature, more electrical energy is converted into heat energy to warm the water. The hotter the wash, the more electrical energy is needed, so usage of energy increases.

Question 36

3 marks

Ali conducted an experiment using the set-up as shown below. He wanted to find out the mass of the ball required to move marble M to hit the bell. Ball A was released to hit marble M. Marble M moved in the direction as shown. He repeated the experiment with Balls B and C and recorded his results.

BallMass of Ball (g)Height moved by marble MDid the bell ring?
A15050Yes
B12045No
C9040No
(a) What is the relationship between the mass of the ball and the height moved by marble M? [1]
(b) The bell did not ring when Ball A was released from a lower position on the set-up. Explain this observation in terms of energy conversion. [2]

Answer

(a) As the mass of the ball increases, the height moved by marble M increases.
(b) When Ball A is released from a lower position, it has less gravitational potential energy. This is converted into less kinetic energy, so the ball hits marble M with less energy. Marble M gains too little energy to move high enough to ring the bell.

Question 37

5 marks

The diagram below shows a machine that is used to demolish buildings. The metal ball swings and hits the building to break it. The higher the ball swings, the greater the damage done.

(a) The metal ball is pulled backwards before it is released to hit the building. Fill in the boxes below to show the energy conversion as the metal ball is swung from positions A to B. [2]
(b) Explain how swinging the ball higher will cause more damage to the building. [2]
(c) Other than swinging the ball higher, what change can be made to the ball to cause more damage to the building? [1]

Answer

(a) The completed energy conversion as the ball swings from A to B:
(b) When the ball is swung higher, it gains more gravitational potential energy. As it swings down this is converted into more kinetic energy, so the ball hits the building with more force, causing more damage.
(c) Increase the mass of the metal ball (a heavier ball has more energy and hits with greater force).

Question 38

4 marks

A “Maglev” train is a special train that floats a few centimetres above the track while it is moving. This is made possible by the use of very strong magnets.

(a) Explain what causes the gap between the magnets. [1]
(b) Explain why “Maglev” trains travel much faster than normal trains. [2]

The “Maglev” train cannot hold beyond a certain amount of weight for it to function. Sasha made a model of a “Maglev” train as shown below.

(c) Suggest what she should measure to find out the maximum weight the model train can hold. [1]

Answer

(a) The magnets on the train and the magnets on the track have like poles facing each other, so they repel each other, pushing the train up and creating the gap.
(b) Because the train floats above the track, there is no contact between the train and the track. This greatly reduces friction, so the train can travel much faster than a normal train that rubs against its rails.
(c) She should keep adding weights and measure the weight on the train just before the gap (distance) between the train and track becomes zero (i.e. just before the train touches the track).

Question 39

4 marks

A man is trying to push a box as shown.

(a) Name the forces, P and Q, acting on the box. [2]
(b) The man found that no matter how hard he pushed, he was not able to move the box. Explain why. [1]
(c) Suggest one way to help the man move the box easily in the direction as shown. [1]

Answer

(a) P: Frictional force (acting against the push). Q: Gravitational force (the weight of the box acting downwards).
(b) The force the man exerts on the box is not large enough to overcome the friction between the box and the floor, so the forces are balanced and the box does not move.
(c) Add wheels (rollers) to the bottom of the box to reduce friction, making it easier to move. (Alternatively, place the box on a smoother surface.)

Question 40

3 marks

Jun Xiang threw a piece of plasticine on a noticeboard as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Name the force that caused the plasticine to drop on the ground. [1]
(b) Give two effects of force on the plasticine caused by the throwing. [2]

Answer

(a) Gravitational force (gravity) pulled the plasticine down to the ground.
(b) The throwing caused (1) a change in the shape of the plasticine (it flattened/squashed on hitting the board) and (2) a change in the speed (and direction) of the plasticine.
